role_summary: SNCC organizer who became the Selma, Alabama project director in 1963 and a leader of the Arkansas project; later a folklorist with the Smithsonian Institution and a co-founder of the Mississippi Delta Blues Festival.
ai_reading: Ranked by shared-passage count, Long's nearest neighbors in this corpus are James Oscar Jones (entry 57, count 57), Esther M. A. Terry (entry 40, count 45), Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29, count 42), the John Dudley joint interview (entry 64, count 42), and Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 37). The embedding is reading a childhood signature, not a movement one: the only testimony in his file is a long account of growing up in Durham's Hayti district as the son of an AME Zion presiding elder, so the vector clusters him with other interviewees narrating rural-to-town Black churched upbringings rather than with fellow SNCC field staff. Two axis positions cut against that surface. On the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis he reads +0.72 toward Armed Self-Defense, joining the catalog's recurring inversion in which church-formed voices land on the self-defense pole; and on the Sacred versus Secular axis he sits at +0.60, leaning secular, even though the transcript is saturated with his father's ministry and the business of the church. The influence graph supplies what his own words omit: five interviewees name him, led by James Oscar Jones (count 10) and Euvester Simpson (count 8), both placing him in SNCC's Arkansas and Mississippi work that his captured testimony never reaches.
biographical_paragraph: Worth Westmoreland Long Jr. was born on January 15, 1936, at Duke Hospital in Durham, North Carolina, the son of Gertrude Watson Long and the Reverend William Worth Long Sr., a presiding elder of the Durham district of the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church [src: 1]. He grew up on the edge of Hayti, Durham's self-sustaining Black business district, and was taught Pan-African consciousness and Black self-sufficiency in his father's house [src: 2, 3]. After two years in the United States Air Force stationed in Korea and Japan, he enrolled at Philander Smith College in Little Rock in 1962 and moved into Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee organizing, helping lead sit-ins at Little Rock's Woolworth's and Walgreen's lunch counters and being arrested in June 1963 [src: 2, 4]. He became SNCC's Selma, Alabama project director in 1963, succeeding Bernard and Colia Lafayette [src: 2, 4]. From the early 1970s he worked as a folklorist with the Smithsonian Institution, co-directing the Mississippi Folklife Project with the photographer Roland Freeman, helping found the Mississippi Delta Blues Festival, and producing the radio history of the movement "Will the Circle Be Unbroken?" [src: 2, 5]. He died on May 8, 2025, in Atlanta at the age of eighty-nine [src: 5, 6]. His oral history is held by the Library of Congress as entry 131 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]; other interviewees in the corpus place him in that movement work, including James Oscar Jones (entry 57) and Euvester Simpson (entry 41).

interview_snippets.speaker: Worth W. Long
interview_snippets.lead_in: Long recalls how his father, an AME Zion preacher, told family stories during the farm labor the two shared:
interview_snippets.quote: My father would talk about it when he took me out to work me to death on the farm.

interview_snippets.speaker: Worth W. Long
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the rural roots his parents carried into Durham, a saying he says he later reworked into a statement about Africa:
interview_snippets.quote: there's a saying that you can take black folk from the country. But you can't take the country from black folk.

interview_snippets.speaker: Worth W. Long
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing how he understood the state of North Carolina building a separate Black law school rather than admit Floyd McKissick, Long set his priorities apart from integration:
interview_snippets.quote: The question of integration at that time was not one of my major concerns. My concern was having access to something that could prepare you for life.

interview_snippets.speaker: Worth W. Long
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on a white filling-station boy who had no language for how to address his father, Long drew a lesson about how change happens:
interview_snippets.quote: But change, I think, comes from engagement.

interview_snippets.speaker: James Oscar Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: James Oscar Jones recalls first meeting Long and the white SNCC organizer Bill Hansen when they came to organize students in Arkansas:
interview_snippets.quote: When, when we met, Worth Long talked about he had been involved in labor and union groups a lot.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether she had come across Long in Mississippi, Euvester Simpson remembered the work that defined his later career:
interview_snippets.quote: And Worth Long was really involved in... he was an oral historian. He liked to go out in the countryside and just talk to regular people and find out what was going on.
